data.title: Verizon: Not Overly Incentivized To Stay Bullish Ahead Of Q1 Earnings
data.description:
A Steady Start To The Year

While it’s still early days, 2025 so far, is proving to be a fairly decent year for the shareholders of the telecommunications giant- Verizon Communications Inc. (NYSE:VZ). While the large-cap equity benchmark has collapsed by 14%, and other telecommunication stocks have fallen by a lesser threshold (on average). VZ has bucked the trend and eked out positive gains of over 6% on a YTD basis.

Admittedly, a lot can change over a two-week time frame (more so in the current environment with the VIX behaving the way it is), but we suspect VZ’s ability to keep up this bout of alpha, will be more keenly tested on the 22nd of April (exactly two weeks from today) when it releases its Q1 earnings.

If you’re contemplating a position in VZ ahead of this event, here are some of the important sub-plots to consider.

Earnings Expectations

Given VZ’s recent track record in meeting street estimates, we suspect stakeholders of Verizon won’t necessarily be in the most intrepid state. To be clear, the uncertainty isn’t linked to the bottom-line performance, where VZ has met or beaten estimates for the last 10 consecutive quarters (equating to an average EPS beat of 1.4% per quarter). Rather, it’s because of the topline track record, where VZ has been rather inconsistent, beating estimates only 50% of the time over the last 10 quarters. All in all, VZ is a company that has managed an average revenue miss of -0.2% per quarter, so if it can do better than this in Q1, it should be taken well by the market.

For the upcoming Q1, the two key headline metrics to track are a revenue figure of $33.3B, and a normalized EPS of $1.15.

Another reason not to get too gung-ho about VZ is because Q1 is their seasonally weakest quarter (if VZ meets consensus on the topline, it would still represent growth of less than 1% YoY, lower than +1.6% growth seen in Q4-24), and this year, the pressure has been amplified by a generally weak market (as opposed to 9m p.a. net postpaid additions seen over the last 3 years, this year, the industry as a whole, is only likely to see net additions to the tune of 8-8.5m (includes both customers and businesses), which would be around 8% lower than last year), and “ aggressive” competitive promotional activity by the likes of AT&T (T) and T-Mobile US, Inc (TMUS) since the turn of 2025. VZ not only abandoned the promotional game at the end of the holiday season, but also resorted to further price-ups in January, on the back of price-ups in the latter half of 2024 ( 4 in total last year). Because of all this, churn is likely to be elevated ( 3-5bps YoY) and total postpaid net adds are unlikely to grow, after a very strong Q4 where they saw 568K of net adds (note also that traditionally VZ has witnessed net losses as opposed to net additions).

VZ management can help ameliorate the likely weak subscriber additions of Q1, by talking up its prospects for the rest of the year (expect things like their perk heavy myPlan, and growing personalization efforts for customers to be highlighted). It will also be interesting to see if they can keep growing their consumer ARPA at over 4% because of these price increases (4.2% in Q4)

To be fair to VZ, their recent pricing actions should still add about $1B of incremental revenue this year, and wireless services revenue growth for the FY is still expected to come in at respectable levels of 2-2.8% YoY (although still lower than the 3.1% YoY growth seen in FY24). Also, the level of device upgrades this year is expected to be much better than last year, which was one of the weakest in its recent history. Average device lives have now crossed the 40-month landmark and will need to be replaced, although we suspect most of the upgrade benefits will take place in H2 as opposed to Q1 (for the whole year, VZ is budgeting for a mid-single-digit increase on upgrades).

VZ’s ability to generate decent free cash flow too will be scrutinized, given its humungous debt servicing burden ($144B of total debt, with 82% of this unsecured, equating to 2.3x VZ’s EBITDA) and dividend commitments (it has been growing its dividends annually for 18 years on the trot). Once again, don’t expect Q1 to be particularly resplendent on the operating cash flow front, but even for the FY, the FCF outlook relative to last year, isn’t the greatest. Last year’s FCF of close to $20B, did benefit from one-time effects to the tune of $1.4B on a net basis (positive contribution of $2b from the Vertical bridge tower transaction and negative contribution of $0.6B related to severance payments) which won’t quite be there this year. All in all, the FY25 FCF is expected to dip by around 9% to $18bn (mid-point of guidance), even as CAPEX commitments (in high-speed fiber, AI, wireless networks, etc.) step up by around $1bn YoY this year to $18bn (mid-point).

What does this mean for the dividend and the debt burden? Well, assuming they don’t buy back any shares (they intend to resume buybacks only when debt to EBITDA drops to below 2.25x), and carry on with their usual 2% dividend hike p.a., they could be looking at a cash dividend outflow of $11.5bn for the year, which their expected FCF (of $18bn) should cover by 1.6x. VZ could still have $6.5bn which it can then use to bring down its debt, although this will still only be a pittance (they’ll only be bringing down less than 5% of their total debt).

Closing Thought - Is Verizon A Good Buy Ahead of The Q1 Results?

As suggested earlier, Q1-25 is unlikely to be the strongest quarter for VZ, and the charting and valuation backdrop too isn’t the most attractive either.

Verizon’s weekly price movements (seen in the image below) over the last two years, appear to be taking the firm of a rising wedge pattern, with two converging trendlines pointing to some maturation in the uptrend as buying momentum wanes. For the uninitiated, the rising wedge typically points to the risk of a reversal.

We also acknowledge VZ’s current dividend yield of over 6% is still quite attractive, but it is not hugely better than what other integrated telecommunication services stocks across the world currently offer (industry average of 6.2%).

Meanwhile, the relative strength charts using the telecommunications universe at the base helps identify suitable rotational opportunities within this terrain. As opposed to conditions earlier in the year, VZ’s relative strength ratio is now no longer trading well below its mean, but around 7% higher, dampening the incentive for investors to rotate into this.

At current price levels, VZ’s forward valuations are also not exactly cheap either. As opposed to its rolling P/E average of 8.4x over the last 5 years, the stock is now priced at a high-single-digit percentage premium.

One can appreciate the merit of paying that premium if VZ was on course to deliver exceptional earnings growth that would imply a PEG (Price-to-earnings growth) ratio of around 1x or thereabouts. However, that’s not quite what you’re going to see this year, with expected EPS growth of less than 2% (implying a relatively high PEG of over 5x)!

Thus, to conclude, we are now revising our rating on VZ from a Buy to a Hold.

Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that when shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For up-to-date fund performance, including performance current to the most recent month end, please visit our website at GuggenheimInvestments.com. Load performance reflects maximum sales charges or contingent deferred sales charges (CDSC) as applicable. A Class has a maximum sales charge of 4.00%.

The referenced fund is offered in multiple share classes. Please read the prospectus for information on fees, expenses and holding periods that may apply to each class.

  • Inception date of benchmark return is that of the fund’s oldest share class.
  • The Net Expense Ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver by the Adviser through February 1, 2025, to limit the ordinary operating expenses of the fund.
  • The Adjusted Expense Ratio (“Adjusted”) is based on the Net Expense Ratio, but excludes certain investment expenses, such as interest expense from borrowings and repurchase agreements and dividend expense from investments on short sales.
  • Benchmark-Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index.

Unless otherwise noted, data is as of 12.31.2024. Data subject to change. Partial year returns are cumulative. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends. The referenced index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Index performance does not reflect transaction costs, fees, or expenses. Index data source: FundStation.

Performance Review

The Fund (Institutional Class) returned -0.90 percent for the fourth quarter, outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, by 0.32 percent. This brought the net year-to-date absolute and relative performance to 3.01 percent and 1.96 percent, respectively. Longer-duration products detracted from performance as rates sold off during the quarter, with tax-exempt agency commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) and zero-coupon bonds giving back a portion of their third quarter outperformance. Among the most heavily represented states in the portfolio, New York bonds contributed the most to total return, while those from Virginia and Tennessee detracted from returns for the period.

Strategy and Positioning

The market began 2025 with expectations of a large new issue calendar, while discussions ramped up in Washington D.C. over tax regime changes and their potential impact on municipal issuers’ access to tax-exempt financing.

Valuations remained tight despite widespread uncertainty. We believe traditional high quality sectors, such as general obligation property tax bonds and utilities, do not offer attractive risk/reward at current muni/Treasury yield ratios.

Instead, we are looking for solid credits within higher beta sectors, including affordable housing, charter schools, and continuing care retirement communities (CCRCs).

QTD Contributors / Detractors

Longer-duration products detracted as rates sold off during the quarter. Tax exempt Agency CMBS detracted. Zero-coupon bonds detracted. New York bonds contributed to performance, while Virginia and Tennessee detracted.

Economic and Market Review

The U.S. economy has good momentum heading into 2025, but the policy outlook from Washington elevates uncertainty. Recent economic data have been solid, with fourth quarter real gross domestic product (GDP) on track for about 2 percent annualized growth. The outlook for consumer spending remains positive, supported by healthy growth in inflation-adjusted labor income and a wealth effect driven by rising asset prices. Financial conditions have also turned more supportive as credit growth is reaccelerating and optimism about artificial intelligence induces a positive outlook for capex. Disinflationary progress has stalled a bit in recent months, but fundamentals point to a further slowdown in inflation as wage pressures and housing inflation ease further. With the new administration taking office, investors want to know how the evolving policy landscape will alter the outlook. In the immediate future, we expect a boost to both consumer and business sentiment, aided by expectations of deregulation and further tax cuts. Post-election surveys have already shown increased optimism about the outlook, which could support consumption, investment, and hiring in coming months. Looking beyond the immediate sentiment boost, the outlook becomes more uncertain and depends on the ultimate policy mix of the new administration. Extension of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) would prevent a fiscal drag, but we see limited scope for new tax cuts as the fiscal backdrop has worsened. Broad tariffs could weigh on growth and push up prices, though we expect more targeted tariffs will ultimately be used.

All together, we see moderate growth in the U.S. economy in 2025 as these policy shifts play out. Economic fundamentals remain solid, with strong household and corporate balance sheets. The Federal Reserve (Fed) will likely ease policy further toward a neutral setting, but tariffs could slow the pace of rate cuts by interrupting the disinflationary trend. The U.S. economy should remain a global outperformer, though we expect continued bifurcation across sectors, particularly as new policies begin to have an impact.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Please see page 2 for additional performance information.

Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors.

The fund will be significantly affected by events that affect the municipal bond market, which could include unfavorable legislative or political developments and adverse changes in the financial conditions of state and municipal issuers or the federal government in case it provides financial support to the municipality. Income from municipal bonds held by the fund could be declared taxable because of changes in tax laws. The fund may invest in securities that generate taxable income. A portion of the fund’s otherwise tax-exempt dividends may be taxable to those shareholders subject to the alternative minimum tax.

Certain sectors of the municipal bond market have special risks that can affect them more significantly than the market as a whole. Because many municipal instruments are issued to finance similar projects, conditions in these industries can significantly affect the fund and the overall municipal market.

  • Municipalities currently experience budget shortfalls, which could cause them to default on their debt and thus subject the fund to unforeseen losses.
  • Like other funds that hold bonds and other fixed-income investments, the fund’s market value will change in response to interest rate changes and market conditions, among other factors. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa.
  • The fund’s exposure to high-yield securities may subject the fund to greater volatility.
  • When market conditions are deemed appropriate, the fund will leverage to the full extent permitted by its investment policies and restrictions and applicable law. Leveraging will exaggerate the effect on net asset value of any increase or decrease in the market value of the fund’s portfolio.
  • The fund may invest in derivative instruments, which may be more volatile and less liquid, increasing the risk of loss when compared to traditional securities. Certain of the derivative instruments are also subject to the risks of counterparty default and adverse tax treatment.
  • Instruments and strategies (such as reverse repurchase agreements, unfunded commitments, tender option bonds and borrowings) may expose the fund to many of the same risks as investments in derivatives and may provide leveraged exposure to a particular investment, which will magnify any gains or losses on those investments.
  • The fund’s investments in other investment vehicles subject the fund to those risks and expenses affecting the investment vehicle.
  • The fund’s investments in foreign securities carry additional risks when compared to U.S. securities, due to the impact of diplomatic, political or economic developments in the country in question (investments in emerging markets securities are generally subject to an even greater level of risks).
  • Investments in syndicated bank loans generally offer a floating interest rate and involve special types of risks.
  • The fund is subject to active trading risks that may increase volatility and impact its ability to achieve its investment objective.
  • You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares.
  • It is important to note that the fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. government.
  • Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

Index Definition. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is the flagship measure of the U.S. municipal tax-exempt investment grade bond market.

This material is not intended as a recommendation or as investment advice of any kind, including in connection with rollovers, transfers, and distributions. Such material is not provided in a fiduciary capacity, may not be relied upon for or in connection with the making of investment decisions, and does not constitute a solicitation of an offer to buy or sell securities. data.title: Is Now The Time To Invest In Perspective Therapeutics
data.description:
What Happened

Shares of Perspective Therapeutics, Inc. (NYSE:CATX) have declined considerably over the past year. The sharpest move downward happened in November 2024, following the company’s interim data update for its lead candidate, [212Pb]VMT-α-NET.

While Perspective released what we would describe as encouraging safety data, including no dose-limiting toxicities and several cases of tumor shrinkage, we think the market had expected a stronger outcome on the efficacy side. Eight of nine patients experienced disease control, but only one confirmed RECIST response was observed. We believe that distinction contributed to the negative reaction.

As such, we think the update caused Wall Street to reassess the timeline for development of [212Pb]VMT-α-NET, as well as the near-term value potential of Perspective's pipeline. For reasons discussed in this article, we initiate a Hold rating on the stock.

Pipeline Status

The company's pipeline revolves around [212Pb]VMT-α-NET, which is being developed for SSTR2-positive neuroendocrine tumors. The agent is under Phase 1/2a study. As of February 28, 2025, 30 patients have been enrolled. All of these patients are expected to have completed treatment and received post-treatment scans by the second half of 2025. At which point, we may get a better understanding of the drug's efficacy.

In our view, the safety profile of the agent is quite strong. No patient in the study experienced dose-limiting toxicities, grade 4 or 5 adverse events, or had to end treatment due to side effects or other safety concerns. The blood-related side effects (e.g., anemia) reported from the study were only at the milder grades 1 and 2. If the same safety profile holds up in larger cohorts, that could help build confidence around tolerability.

Efficacy, unfortunately, has been a different story. In terms of antitumor activity, there was only one confirmed RECIST response and two unconfirmed responses among the seven patients analyzed. While this points to early biological activity, we believe the low number of confirmed responses reinforces that the company has not yet demonstrated clinical proof of concept. Hence, a lack of efficacy is our biggest concern regarding [212Pb]VMT-α-NET.

In addition, the company presented data from two studies investigating [212Pb]VMT-α-NET in advanced NETs and medullary thyroid cancer. These studies may help further define the agent’s safety window and efficacy.

Perspective is also developing PSMA-targeted alpha radiotherapies in prostate cancer. These programs are preclinical, and, in our view, the timelines for development are not entirely clear. Based on what the company has disclosed to date, we think it is unlikely that clinical trials in this setting will begin before 2026.

Financial Highlights

For the full year 2024, the company reported having roughly $227 million in cash, equivalents, and short-term investments. The company lost roughly $79.3 million, of which approximately $41.6 million was related to R&D. The company last conducted a secondary offering in May 2024, when it priced $80 million of common stock and pre-funded warrants at a price of $1.51 per share.

The company expects to have a cash runway through late 2026. Thus, we view the risk of a return to the capital markets in the near term to be low. That being said, we think any significant, unexpected pipeline developments, such as clinical failures or new drug introductions, would shorten that runway considerably.

Near-Term Catalysts

We view the most significant near-term catalyst as continued enrollment and potential interim data from the company's ongoing Phase 1/2a trial. The company has indicated that additional information may be available in the second half of 2025.

The next major inflection point for the company will likely be data showing that [212Pb]VMT-α-NET yields a large amount of RECIST responses among patients in the trial. If safety remains clean and RECIST responses increase, investor sentiment should turn positive, and the stock should rise.

Risk/Reward Analysis

We believe the stock’s recent decline largely reflects a reset in expectations. At current prices, the market capitalization is still less than the company’s reported cash balance, which lends minimal valuation support. Coupled with the uncertainty regarding the efficacy of [212Pb]VMT-α-NET, we see a new investment in the stock to be high risk.

If the company delivers clearer signals of efficacy in larger cohorts, we think sentiment could improve and the stock should trade significantly higher. On the other hand, if timelines extend or enrollment slows, we cannot rule out further downside.

For now, we remain neutral to slightly bearish on Perspective. The platform is early and shows some promise, but we have concerns about efficacy in light of the available data. The company's valuation is still significantly above cash levels, indicating little valuation support. That being said, there is, in our opinion, a low risk of near-term dilution. Further, we do not see a near-term catalyst justifying an outright bullish rating.

Conclusion

The company's prospects appear to be almost completely dependent on the success of [212Pb]VMT-α-NET. At this point, while we view the agent as demonstrably safe, the jury is still out regarding its efficacy. Obviously, proving the agent is efficacious is critical for the company, as it will determine whether the agent is commercially viable.

In our opinion, any upside remains dependent on stronger efficacy data for [212Pb]VMT-α-NET, a partnership, or both. We do not have enough data at this time to warrant a bullish stance on Perspective. However, if efficacy data are strong, the stock should trade exponentially higher and potentially reclaim pre-November 2024 levels. For these reasons, we rate the stock a Hold.

For up-to-date fund performance, including performance current to the most recent month end, please visit our website at GuggenheimInvestments.com.

The referenced fund is offered in multiple share classes. Please read the prospectus for information on fees, expenses and holding periods that may apply to each class.

  • Inception date of benchmark return is that of the fund’s oldest share class.
  • The Net Expense Ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver by the Adviser through February 1, 2025, to limit the ordinary operating expenses of the fund.
  • The Adjusted Expense Ratio (“Adjusted”) is based on the Net Expense Ratio, but excludes certain investment expenses, such as interest expense from borrowings and repurchase agreements and dividend expense from investments on short sales.
  • Benchmark-Bloomberg 1-3 Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index.

Unless otherwise noted, data is as of 12.31.2024. Data subject to change. Partial year returns are cumulative. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends. The referenced index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Index performance does not reflect transaction costs, fees, or expenses. Index data source: FundStation.

Performance Review

The Fund (Institutional Class) finished the fourth quarter up 1.21 percent (net), outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg 1-3 Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index, by 0.02 percent. This brought the net 2024 calendar year absolute and relative performance to 7.01 percent and 1.69 percent, respectively. Outperformance was driven largely by sector and security selection as well as the Fund’s carry advantage over the benchmark.

Duration modestly detracted from absolute returns as interest rates rose following stronger-than-expected economic data and in response to the U.S. election results.

The Fund benefitted from an overweight allocation to structured credit as the sector broadly outperformed other fixed-income sectors, such as corporate credit, due to more pronounced spread tightening. High-yield (HY) and investment-grade (IG) corporates were key contributors to performance and outperformed respective indices for each sector by 0.8 percent and 0.7 percent, respectively.

Strategy and Positioning

The Fund maintained a relatively neutral overall risk positioning, reflecting a balanced approach focusing on capital preservation while selectively capitalizing on opportunities across various higher quality credit segments. Positioning favors high carry, shorter duration instruments, such as senior tranches within non-Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS), esoteric asset-backed securities (ABS), and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs).

Securitized credit is the largest allocation within the Fund, representing approximately 45 percent. The allocation is diversified across CLOs, RMBS/CMBS, and ABS collateral types. Yields across these sectors are at notably attractive levels versus corporate credit.

The Fund maintained an above-average allocation to enhanced cash investments to have dry powder to take advantage of potential selloffs in risk assets.

QTD Contributors / Detractors

Carry contributed 1.4 percent to absolute return and 0.3 percent to relative return. Spread tightening across structured credit made a strong positive contribution to return. Duration detracted by 0.5 percent on an absolute and relative basis.

Economic and Market Review

The U.S. economy has good momentum heading into 2025, but the policy outlook from Washington elevates uncertainty. Recent economic data have been solid, with fourth quarter real gross domestic product (GDP) on track for about 2 percent annualized growth. The outlook for consumer spending remains positive, supported by healthy growth in inflation-adjusted labor income and a wealth effect driven by rising asset prices. Financial conditions have also turned more supportive as credit growth is reaccelerating and optimism about artificial intelligence induces a positive outlook for capex. Disinflationary progress has stalled a bit in recent months, but fundamentals point to a further slowdown in inflation as wage pressures and housing inflation ease further. With a new administration in office, investors want to know how the evolving policy landscape will alter the outlook. In the immediate future, we expect a boost to both consumer and business sentiment, aided by expectations of deregulation and further tax cuts. Post-election surveys have already shown increased optimism about the outlook, which could support consumption, investment, and hiring in coming months. Looking beyond the immediate sentiment boost, the outlook becomes more uncertain and depends on the ultimate policy mix of the new administration. Extension of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) would prevent a fiscal drag, but we see limited scope for new tax cuts as the fiscal backdrop has worsened. Broad tariffs could weigh on growth and push up prices, though we expect more targeted tariffs will ultimately be used.

All together, we see moderate growth in the U.S. economy in 2025 as these policy shifts play out. Economic fundamentals remain solid, with strong household and corporate balance sheets. The Federal reserve (Fed) will likely ease policy further toward a neutral setting, but tariffs could slow the pace of rate cuts by interrupting the disinflationary trend. The U.S. economy should remain a global outperformer, though we expect continued bifurcation across sectors, particularly as new policies begin to have an impact.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Please see page 2 for additional performance information.

Bps (basis points): One basis point is equal to 0.01%. Carry: The difference between the cost of financing an asset and the interest received on that asset. Dry powder: highly liquid assets, such as cash or money market instruments, that can be invested when more attractive investment opportunities arise. Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors. In general, the value of a fixed-income security falls when interest rates rise and rises when interest rates fall. Longer term bonds are more sensitive to interest rate changes and subject to greater volatility than those with shorter maturities. During periods of declining rates, the interest rates on floating rate securities generally reset downward and their value is unlikely to rise to the same extent as comparable fixed rate securities.

Investors in asset-backed securities, including collateralized loan obligations (“CLOs”), generally receive payments that are part interest and part return of principal. These payments may vary based on the rate loans are repaid. Some asset-backed securities may have structures that make their reaction to interest rates and other factors difficult to predict, making their prices volatile and they are subject to liquidity and valuation risk.

High yield and unrated debt securities are at a greater risk of default than investment grade bonds and may be less liquid, which may increase volatility.

When market conditions are deemed appropriate, the fund will leverage to the full extent permitted by its investment policies and restrictions and applicable law. Leveraging will exaggerate the effect on net asset value of any increase or decrease in the market value of the fund’s portfolio.

The fund may invest in derivative instruments, which may be more volatile and less liquid, increasing the risk of loss when compared to traditional securities. Certain of the derivative instruments are also subject to the risks of counterparty default and adverse tax treatment.

Instruments and strategies (such as borrowing transactions and reverse repurchase agreements) may provide leveraged exposure to a particular investment, which will magnify any gains or losses on those investments.

Investments in reverse repurchase agreements expose the fund to the many of the same risks as investments in derivatives.

The fund’s investments in other investment vehicles subject the fund to those risks and expenses affecting the investment vehicle.

The fund’s investments in foreign securities carry additional risks when compared to U.S. securities, due to the impact of diplomatic, political, or economic developments in the country in question (investments in emerging markets securities are generally subject to an even greater level of risks).

Investments in syndicated bank loans generally offer a floating interest rate and involve special types of risks.

The fund’s investments in municipal securities can be affected by events that affect the municipal bond market.

The fund’s investments in real estate securities subject the fund to the same risks as direct investments in real estate, which is particularly sensitive to economic downturns.

The fund’s investments in restricted securities may involve financial and liquidity risk.

You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares.

It is important to note that the fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

Index Definition. The Bloomberg 1-3 Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index measures the U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate, nominal debt issued by the U.S. Treasury with maturities of one to three months.

This material is not intended as a recommendation or as investment advice of any kind, including in connection with rollovers, transfers, and distributions. It contains the fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges, expenses, and other information, which should be considered carefully before investing. Obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus (if available) at GuggenheimInvestments.com.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

data.title: Bath & Body Works: Rating Downgrade On Macro Turmoil And Potential EPS Miss
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to Bath & Body Works Inc. (NYSE:BBWI) because I wanted to see tangible data points of BBWI's successful turnaround. Specifically, I wanted to see growth accelerating back to high-single-digits [HSD] percentage before I gave it a buy rating. I have now downgraded my rating from hold to sell because of the recent macro developments and that BBWI may miss its EPS guidance, causing further de-rating of its stock’s valuation.

Macro turmoil

At a time when BBWI is still working to turn around the business through its various efforts—i.e., product positioning, TikTok Shop launch, etc.—I think recent macro developments have almost completely eliminated the possibility of a successful turnaround to high-single-digit growth this year. The biggest negative event that convinced me BBWI will have a very rough year ahead is Trump’s decision to implement tariffs of huge magnitude. This has dramatically changed the macro situation, as there is a real risk of a trade war—China has already retaliated with 34% reciprocal tariffs, and Europe has plans to do so as well.

This has a direct impact on BBWI because these tariffs weigh on consumers’ ability to spend—tariffs are likely to be passed through to consumers as retailers hike prices to offset tariffs. More importantly, in times of uncertainty, consumers cut discretionary spending first, and BBWI products belong to the discretionary category. We know for a fact that consumers are not confident about the macro situation, and hence, I see a very strong likelihood that consumers will cut discretionary spending.

Although management has the impact of China tariffs in their guidance (note this guide was given on 27th February 2025), they have explicitly mentioned that they have not included other potential tariffs in the guidance (which includes the Liberation Day tariffs). As of the time of writing, there is also no certainty that Trump will not retaliate to the 34% tariffs implemented by China. If he does, I see a significant room for downside surprise (i.e., BBWI to miss guidance by a big margin).

Reliance on promotions to capture traffic

Another data point that got me very worried about BBWI’s FY25 outlook is that average unit retail [AUR] declined in 4Q24. Even if we adjust for mix impact, AUR is still down low-single-digits percentage, and this worries me because it suggests BBWI is leaning heavily on promotions to drive traffic (traffic saw positive y/y in 4Q24). Here is the implication: if BBWI needs to rely on promotions to drive positive traffic during a peak sales season, then it would very likely need to rely more on promotions in the current macro environment where consumer demand is much worse.

While I was bullish on BBWI’s product newness strategy to win demand in my last write-up, this thesis only works if BBWI is not relying on using promotions and discounts. Viewed from another angle, the level of demand due to product newness is seemingly not able to offset the broader demand deterioration, and as such, BBWI needs to rely on promotions. If product newness doesn’t work, and the macro conditions continue to erode, in my view, BBWI is very likely to see demand continue to decline.

Inventory Buildup Adds Another Layer of Uncertainty

Another subtle but important red flag that could cause BBWI to report poor earnings is the growth in inventory levels. Although management noted inventory levels as “lean,” I struggle to reconcile that comment since inventories grew 3.4% y/y in 4Q24. Notably, the inventory-to-sales growth spread has risen back to 8%. While this was not a level that BBWI has not hit before (the last time this metric was at 8% was in 2Q24, and the metric flipped to -5% in 3Q24), the situation is very different today. Demand is not as strong as it was last year, and my worry is that if demand really falls as I expect, BBWI may have to discount more aggressively to clear inventories. This would further pressure merchandise margin and effectively push out any hopes for positive EPS growth.

Valuation

Aside from the macro and fundamental weaknesses, I am also bearish on BBWI because consensus doesn’t seem to have priced in the impact of new tariffs in their estimates (Note: Bloomberg’s FY26 is actually FY25). Consensus near-term EPS still follows management guidance, which was announced before the recent sweeping tariffs announcements. As such, while BBWI forward PE is at 7.5x, on an adjusted basis (if consensus were to adjust their EPS estimates downwards), BBWI forward PE is higher than where it is today. I do not know the exact impact of the recent tariffs, but if the tariff situation does not resolve soon, the only possible outcome I see is: BBWI misses consensus estimates and revise down guidance, and this should lead to consensus revising estimates and multiples to go down even more.

Investment Risk

BBWI new product collaborations—like the Disney Princess collection—could perform much better than I expected, so much that it actually offsets all the demand headwinds I mentioned. This may help BBWI to outperform its 1Q25 and FY25 guidance. Additionally, if BBWI does not need to rely on promotions to drive demand, or impacts from tariffs is less impactful, demand and earnings growth may outperform my expectations.

Conclusion

All in all, I believe the risk setup for BBWI has changed meaningfully since my last update. AUR is no longer growing, BBWI is seemingly relying on promotions to drive growth, and inventory levels are trending back up. Management guidance also did not include the recent sweeping tariffs and other potential tariffs. As such, I see a big risk to BBWI missing consensus estimates. Until there’s more clarity on demand growth, I would think the stock will continue to trade at a steep discount to its historical average, as such, I give a sell rating.

data.title: CoreWeave's Moat Is Just Starting
data.description:
CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) is an AI-native hyperscaler that reimagines computing infrastructure delivery and optimization for the era of large-scale AI. Legacy hyperscalers are built for broad-purpose elasticity, but CoreWeave is architected specifically for performance, tirelessly optimizing latency, GPU performance, and orchestration efficiency.

However, the composition of the growth, with over 70% of revenue coming from long-term contracts, poses a critical risk but offers visibility and operational stability. While early gains are notable, questions remain around sustainability, capital intensity, and customer concentration as the company transitions from expansion to monetization.

Execution, Financial Moats & Stickiness

CoreWeave's 2024 revenue of $1.9 billion, up from just 2022's $16 million, is evidence of business at hyperscale velocity. More significantly, though, is the quality of the revenue. Over 70% of it is from multiyear, fixed-term contracts with AI labs and hyperscalers. This is not temporary usage-based billing, it’s contractual infrastructure-as-a-service with performance and availability SLAs. In its S-1, the company booked $15.1 billion of remaining performance obligations (RPOs)–an uncommon KPI among private cloud players, indicating visibility and customer commitment through 2028.

Its business is focused on GPU orchestration at scale. CoreWeave does not lease H100s; it owns the whole stack, from pre-purchased GPUs to high-throughput, purpose-built data centers to proprietary orchestration software optimizing large language model (LLM) training and inference. Unlike AWS or Google Cloud, which adapted existing layers of the cloud to support AI, CoreWeave builds AI-first.

CoreWeave's rollout is extremely repeatable and modular. It rolls out in weeks, not months, and this is made possible by its arrangements with power procurement organizations and landlords, outlined in its S-1. It selectively leases colocation facilities and makes investments in hardware racks that can ramp and densify infrastructure at high PUE. They are not generic data centers, they are purpose-built for AI, down to network fabrics and cooling.

This allows the company to deliver high-performance computing environments customized to the needs of AI customers, giving it extensive integration points within customers' training pipelines. Because of workload-tuned and infrastructure-level visibility, CoreWeave becomes mission-critical and not merely a vendor.

Moat Dynamics and Competitive Advantage

CoreWeave's moat is not one of share of the market or brand, it's one of coproduction of hardware and software, workload specialization, and lock-in by contract. Hyperscalers can use one-size-fits-most clouds, but CoreWeave tailors its stack to AI-first workloads. That involves bare-metal tuning, tuning of memory locality, lowering cluster interconnect latency, and container orchestration specifically for deep learning.

This technical moat is manifested as performance gaps that are impactful to customers. Internal benchmarks suggest that CoreWeave can shave days off model training timelines through deeper integration and low-latency clustering. Over several hundred executions, that makes all the difference. It’s not cost, it’s time-to-insight and model iteration velocity, and those are drivers of customer stickiness.

Adding to this is the company’s drive-up stack with its acquisition of Weights & Biases, an established AI dev platform. This suggests CoreWeave’s ambition to own the end-to-end AI workflow, from experimentation all the way to scaled inference, moving up the ML lifecycle earlier. It also makes its platform more sticky, as customers now get infrastructure and tooling from one vendor, offering bundling value and switching costs.

There is a significant gap between theoretical GPU performance and actual observed performance in AI workloads, highlighting what CoreWeave calls the “AI Efficiency Gap.” While GPUs can deliver 100% of their designed output, real-world performance in general-purpose cloud environments typically achieves only 35–45%. This inefficiency stems from architectural mismatches, suboptimal networking, and poor workload orchestration.

CoreWeave’s core value proposition lies in closing this gap through its purpose-built AI cloud, offering optimized hardware configurations, custom software stacks, and low-latency interconnects. Bridging this gap translates directly to faster model training, reduced costs, and better resource utilization, key advantages for performance-obsessed AI customers.

Total Addressable Market and Share Opportunity

According to CoreWeave’s S-1, citing Bloomberg Intelligence, the total addressable market for AI compute infrastructure, including AI servers, storage, training compute, cloud workloads, and networking, is projected to grow from $79 billion in 2023 to approximately $399 billion by 2028, representing a 38% CAGR.

Of this, $330 billion will stem from training infrastructure, $49 billion from inference infrastructure, and $20 billion from workload monitoring. CoreWeave is strategically positioned to capture share across all three segments with its end-to-end, AI-optimized stack. This market expansion underscores not just scale but vertical breadth, supporting the company’s deep specialization and long-term platform potential.

Addressable market is not merely training, it’s inference, deployment, and hybrid workloads. When companies move from experimentation to production, they’ll need stable environments and SLA-supported infrastructure and scaling. That’s where CoreWeave can dominate. AWS, Google, and Microsoft can own the volume game, but CoreWeave’s share is high-margin, highly specialized, and high-priority workloads.

Its go-to-market strategy emphasizes wallet share per customer rather than mass reach. With fewer than 100 core customers, it has locked up $15.1B of RPO. That's tens or even hundreds of millions of cumulative spend per customer. As applications of AI increase, CoreWeave is positioned structurally to grow share within existing customers before even addressing long-tail accounts.

From CapEx to Cash Engine: Rethinking CoreWeave’s Premium Valuation

Assessing CoreWeave with standard valuation measures such as GAAP P/E or PEG ratios is misleading regarding its CapEx-intensive, infrastructure-first business model and growth stage. The company is not a SaaS business with margin-incremental characteristics nor a utility business with yield-driven valuation anchors. Rather, CoreWeave is akin to a highly performing infrastructure platform with long-term revenue contracts, multi-year GPU deployment horizons, and initial indications of operating leverage, all of which necessitate a differentiated valuation framework.

On headline multiples, CoreWeave looks pricey: its trailing EV/Sales of 17x is close to 553% above sector-median 2.65x, with EV/EBITDA at 28x, vs. sector-median 15x. This premium, however, depicts not irrational exuberance but a structurally favored business model in which top-line growth is supported by $15.1 billion of performance obligations, much of which will become recognized revenue over 3–5 years. In contrast to most infrastructure plays, CoreWeave is pre-booking use on behalf of already-funded hardware, shortening the interval between investment and monetization.

Its trailing EV/EBIT of 102x looks outrageous, but it's distorted by cyclical reinvestments, primarily GPU purchases, power expansions, and network infrastructure. The better metric is forward EBITDA expansion. The greater portion of compute turning from training into inference workloads will see gross margin growth because of improved amortization of new hardware on pre-bought H100 and GB200 clusters. With well over 70% of revenues coming from long-duration AI contracts, there's a built-in normalization runway after CapEx levels out.

Additionally, CoreWeave's Price/Cash Flow of 8x, which is 48% below the industry median, implies that the market undervalues the company's capacity to cash flow from high-utilization, high-efficiency clusters. This divergence between high EV-based multiples compared to discounted cash flow-based multiples indicates a company transitioning from hypergrowth investment to monetization efficiency.

It's a familiar metric profile for such a company, as we've seen at similar EV/Sales multiples for early Equinix (EQIX) or Arista Networks (ANET), which built out platform infrastructure before reaching scale margins. The closest valuation comparable to CoreWeave is HPC infrastructure companies, cloud-native networking companies, and modular data center companies, not SaaS companies with a software-first orientation. These comparables were historically re-rated as backlogged and turned to the top line, and EBITDA margins eclipsed the 25–30% barrier. If CoreWeave stays on this trajectory, a 20–25x EV/EBITDA multiple is justified. At a 2026 run-rate of $5–6B in revenue and 30% adj. EBITDA margins, the company could produce $1.5–1.8B in EBITDA, implying a possible enterprise value of over $35B, or ~60% higher than today's valuation range.

Ultimately, the company is being valued as a limited, vertically integrated AI infra layer, not on trailing earnings but on realized contracts, GPU monetization horizons, and developer ecosystem capture. The long-term valuation unlock will be not from less expensive compute, however, but from bundling orchestration layers, dev tools (such as Weights & Biases), and inference platforms into its stack, transferring CoreWeave from just being a supplier of infra to being an AI operating system. That's not reflected in multiples yet, one day, however, it may be.

Risks: Capital Intensity and Competitive Pressures

CoreWeave’s business model hinges on substantial capital investments to fund its rapid expansion, particularly GPU procurement and the development of purpose-built data centers. While essential for maintaining its AI-first infrastructure advantage, this strategy makes the company heavily reliant on upfront CapEx. Financing this growth through debt and long-term hardware commitments exposes CoreWeave to interest rate volatility, refinancing risk, and broader capital market conditions.

In a tighter credit environment or amid rising borrowing costs, CoreWeave’s ability to maintain its current growth trajectory could be constrained. This would introduce liquidity pressure and delay key infrastructure deployments, potentially weakening the company’s ability to meet escalating AI demand or keep pace with better-capitalized competitors.

Moreover, as AI infrastructure remains a fast-evolving field, any mismatch between hardware deployments and future workload needs could lead to underutilized assets or costly retrofits. While early scale offers advantages, the long-term success of CoreWeave’s CapEx strategy depends on disciplined execution and ongoing alignment between infrastructure investment and customer demand.

Bottom Line

If execution continues, CoreWeave could play a foundational role in AI compute, but investors should weigh that upside against the firm's dependence on a narrow customer base and evolving market architecture.

Performance displayed represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that when shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For up-to-date fund performance, including performance current to the most recent month end, please visit our website at GuggenheimInvestments.com. Load performance reflects maximum sales charges or contingent deferred sales charges (CDSC) as applicable. A Class has a maximum sales charge of 4.00%.

The referenced fund is offered in multiple share classes. Please read the prospectus for information on fees, expenses and holding periods that may apply to each class.

  • Inception date of benchmark return is that of the fund’s oldest share class.
  • The Net Expense Ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver by the Adviser through February 1, 2025, to limit the ordinary operating expenses of the fund.
  • The Adjusted Expense Ratio (“Adjusted”) is based on the Net Expense Ratio, but excludes certain investment expenses, such as interest expense from borrowings and repurchase agreements and dividend expense from investments on short sales.
  • ICE BofA 3-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index.

Unless otherwise noted, data is as of 12.31.2024. Data subject to change. Partial year returns are cumulative. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends. The referenced index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Index performance does not reflect transaction costs, fees, or expenses. Index data source: FundStation.

Performance Review

The Fund (Institutional Class) finished the fourth quarter up 0.51 percent, underperforming its benchmark, the ICE Bank of America U.S. 3-Month Treasury Bill Index, by -0.66 percent. This brought the 2024 calendar year absolute and relative performance to 7.75 percent and 2.51 percent, respectively. Positive performance was driven by carry (earned income) and sector/security selection, while duration detracted from absolute returns as interest rates rose following stronger-than-expected economic data and the U.S. election results. The Fund benefitted from its structured credit allocation as securitized credit spreads broadly outperformed corporate credit spreads.

Investment-grade (IG) corporates, asset-backed securities (ABS), and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) were key contributors to performance and outperformed respective indices for each sector by 1.0-2.5 percent.

Strategy and Positioning

The Fund maintained a relatively balanced overall risk level and has prioritized diversification, quality, and liquidity over the last several quarters to help protect against heightened market volatility. Corporate credit represents the largest allocation at approximately 50 percent although it has been increasingly shifted towards a higher quality tilt. We remain cautious on the lowest ratings categories where defaults and distressed exchange activity is elevated.

Securitized credit is the second-largest allocation within the Fund, representing approximately 25 percent. The allocation is diversified across CLOs, ABS, and residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) collateral types. Yields across these sectors are at notably attractive levels. Duration positioning remained approximately unchanged at two years, near the long-term average level for the Fund. The Agency MBS allocation was increased during the quarter, with exposure ending at approximately 7 percent of the Fund. The allocation is concentrated in near-production coupon mortgage pools which possess high carry profiles and wide nominal spreads due to heightened interest rate volatility.

The Fund maintained a 10 percent allocation to enhanced cash-like investments to have dry powder to take advantage of potential selloffs in risk assets.

QTD Contributors / Detractors

Carry contributed 1.5 percent to return, while duration detracted by 1.6 percent. Spread tightening across structured credit made a strong contribution to return. IG corporates’ spread tightening continued to make a positive contribution, as did spread tightening across high-yield bonds and bank loans.

Economic and Market Review

The U.S. economy has good momentum heading into 2025, but the policy outlook from Washington elevates uncertainty. Recent economic data have been solid, with fourth quarter real gross domestic product (GDP) on track for about 2 percent annualized growth. The outlook for consumer spending remains positive, supported by healthy growth in inflation-adjusted labor income and a wealth effect driven by rising asset prices. Financial conditions have also turned more supportive as credit growth is reaccelerating and optimism about artificial intelligence induces a positive outlook for capex. Disinflationary progress has stalled a bit in recent months, but fundamentals point to a further slowdown in inflation as wage pressures and housing inflation ease further. With the new administration taking office, investors want to know how the evolving policy landscape will alter the outlook. In the immediate future, we expect a boost to both consumer and business sentiment, aided by expectations of deregulation and further tax cuts. Post-election surveys have already shown increased optimism about the outlook, which could support consumption, investment, and hiring in coming months. Looking beyond the immediate sentiment boost, the outlook becomes more uncertain and depends on the ultimate policy mix of the new administration. Extension of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) would prevent a fiscal drag, but we see limited scope for new tax cuts as the fiscal backdrop has worsened. Broad tariffs could weigh on growth and push up prices, though we expect more targeted tariffs will ultimately be used.

All together, we see moderate growth in the U.S. economy in 2025 as these policy shifts play out. Economic fundamentals remain solid, with strong household and corporate balance sheets. The Federal Reserve (Fed) will likely ease policy further toward a neutral setting, but tariffs could slow the pace of rate cuts by interrupting the disinflationary trend. The U.S. economy should remain a global outperformer, though we expect continued bifurcation across sectors, particularly as new policies begin to have an impact.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Please see page 2 for additional performance information.

Bps (basis points): One basis point is equal to 0.01%. Carry: The difference between the cost of financing an asset and the interest received on that asset. Dry powder: highly liquid assets, such as cash or money market instruments, that can be invested when more attractive investment opportunities arise. Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors.

In general, the value of a fixed-income security falls when interest rates rise and rises when interest rates fall. Longer term bonds are more sensitive to interest rate changes and subject to greater volatility than those with shorter maturities. During periods of declining rates, the interest rates on floating rate securities generally reset downward and their value is unlikely to rise to the same extent as comparable fixed rate securities.

Investors in asset-backed securities, including collateralized loan obligations (“CLOs”), generally receive payments that are part interest and part return of principal. These payments may vary based on the rate loans are repaid. Some asset-backed securities may have structures that make their reaction to interest rates and other factors difficult to predict, making their prices volatile and they are subject to liquidity and valuation risk.

High yield and unrated debt securities are at a greater risk of default than investment grade bonds and may be less liquid, which may increase volatility.

The intrinsic value of the underlying stocks in which the fund invests may never be realized or the stock may decline in value.

When market conditions are deemed appropriate, the fund may leverage to the full extent permitted by its investment policies and restrictions and applicable law. Leveraging will exaggerate the effect on net asset value of any increase or decrease in the market value of the fund’s portfolio.

The use of short selling involves increased risks and costs. You risk paying more for a security than you received from its sale. Theoretically, stocks sold short have the risk of unlimited losses.

The fund may invest in derivative instruments, which may be more volatile and less liquid, increasing the risk of loss when compared to traditional securities. Certain of the derivative instruments are also subject to the risks of counterparty default and adverse tax treatment.

Instruments and strategies (such as borrowing transactions and reverse repurchase agreements) may provide leveraged exposure to a particular investment, which will magnify any gains or losses on those investments.

Investments in reverse repurchase agreements expose the fund to the many of the same risks as investments in derivatives.

The fund’s investments in other investment vehicles subject the fund to those risks and expenses affecting the investment vehicle.

The fund’s investments in foreign securities carry additional risks when compared to U.S. securities, due to the impact of diplomatic, political, or economic developments in the country in question (investments in emerging markets securities are generally subject to an even greater level of risks).

Investments in syndicated bank loans generally offer a floating interest rate and involve special types of risks.

A highly liquid secondary market may not exist for the commodity-linked structured notes the fund invests in, and there can be no assurance that a highly liquid secondary market will develop.

The fund’s exposure to the commodity markets may subject the fund to greater volatility as commodity-linked investments may be affected by changes in overall market movements, commodity index volatility, changes in interest rates, or factors affecting a particular industry or commodity.

The fund’s investments in municipal securities can be affected by events that affect the municipal bond market.

The fund’s investments in real estate securities subject the fund to the same risks as direct investments in real estate, which is particularly sensitive to economic downturns.

The fund’s investments in restricted securities may involve financial and liquidity risk.

You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares.

It is important to note that the fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

Index Definitions. The ICE BofA 3-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index is an unmanaged market index of U.S. Treasury securities maturing in 90 days that assumes reinvestment of all income. The Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index measures the market for USD-denominated non-investment grade, fixed rate, taxable corporate bonds. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody's, Fitch, and S&P is Bal/BB+/ BB+ or below. The index excludes emerging market debt.

This material is not intended as a recommendation or as investment advice of any kind, including in connection with rollovers, transfers, and distributions. Such material is not provided in a fiduciary capacity, may not be relied upon for or in connection with the making of investment decisions, and does not constitute a solicitation of an offer to buy or sell securities. All content has been provided for informational or educational purposes only and is not intended to be and should not be construed as legal or tax advice and/or a legal opinion. Always consult a financial, tax, and/or legal professional regarding your specific situation.

Read the fund’s prospectus and summary prospectus (if available) carefully before investing. It contains the fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges, expenses, and other information, which should be considered carefully before investing. Obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus (if available) at GuggenheimInvestments.com.

The referenced fund is distributed by Guggenheim Funds Distributors, LLC. Guggenheim Investments represents the investment management businesses of Guggenheim Partners, LLC (“Guggenheim”), which includes Guggenheim Partners Investment Management (“GPIM”), the investment advisor to the referenced fund. Guggenheim Funds Distributors, LLC, is affiliated with Guggenheim and GPIM.

Original Post

Unless otherwise noted, data is as of 12.31.2024. Data subject to change. Partial year returns are cumulative. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends. The referenced index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Index performance does not reflect transaction costs, fees, or expenses. Index data source: FundStation.

Performance Review

The Fund (Institutional Class) finished the fourth quarter up 0.28 percent (net), outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond 1-3 Years Index, by 0.30 percent. This brought the net YTD absolute and relative performances to 5.98 percent and 1.60 percent, respectively. Outperformance was driven largely by sector and security selection as well as the Fund’s carry advantage over the benchmark.

Duration detracted from absolute returns as interest rates rose following stronger-than-expected economic data and in response to the U.S. election results.

The Fund benefitted from an overweight allocation to structured credit as the sector broadly outperformed other fixed-income sectors such as corporate credit due to more pronounced spread tightening.

Strategy and Positioning

The Fund continued to position itself roughly at an average risk level relative to its historical range, reflective of the offsetting effects of strong credit fundamentals coupled with tight valuations. Positioning favors higher credit quality forms of carry, shorter duration instruments, such as senior tranches within non-Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS), esoteric asset-backed securities (ABS), and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs). Securitized credit is the largest allocation within the Fund, representing approximately 40 percent. The allocation is well- diversified and consists largely of CLOs as well as various commercial ABS and RMBS subsectors. Yields across these sectors are at notably attractive levels versus corporate credit.

The Fund maintained a slight duration overweight versus the benchmark. The Agency RMBS allocation increased by 2 percent during the quarter, with exposure ending at approximately 17 percent of the Fund. The allocation is concentrated in near-production pools which offer the highest carry profile and the widest nominal spreads and present the most direct route within the coupon stack to monetize the current state of heightened interest rate volatility.

The Fund marginally decreased its exposure to investment-grade corporates to an approximate 25 percent allocation. While index level spreads are compressed, an active primary market offered some opportunities to add marginally to the sector at reasonable pricing concessions.

The Fund maintains an above-average allocation to defensive sectors, such as Treasurys and enhanced cash investments, to serve as dry powder and leave the Fund well-positioned to take advantage of any potential selloffs in risk assets.

QTD Contributors / Detractors

Carry contributed 1.4 percent to absolute return and over 0.1 percent to relative return. Duration detracted by 1.4 percent on an absolute basis and 0.1 percent on a relative basis. Spread tightening across structured credit contributed on an absolute and relative basis. IG corporates’ spread tightening contributed on an absolute and relative basis.

Economic and Market Review

  • The U.S. economy has good momentum heading into 2025, but the policy outlook from Washington elevates uncertainty.
  • Recent economic data have been solid, with fourth quarter real gross domestic product (GDP) on track for about 2 percent annualized growth. The outlook for consumer spending remains positive, supported by healthy growth in inflation-adjusted labor income and a wealth effect driven by rising asset prices.
  • Financial conditions have also turned more supportive as credit growth is reaccelerating and optimism about artificial intelligence induces a positive outlook for capex. Disinflationary progress has stalled a bit in recent months, but fundamentals point to a further slowdown in inflation as wage pressures and housing inflation ease further.
  • With a new presidential administration, investors want to know how the evolving policy landscape will alter the outlook. In the immediate future, we expect a boost to both consumer and business sentiment, aided by expectations of deregulation and further tax cuts. Post-election surveys have already shown increased optimism about the outlook, which could support consumption, investment, and hiring in coming months.
  • Looking beyond the immediate sentiment boost, the outlook becomes more uncertain and depends on the ultimate policy mix of the new administration. Extension of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) would prevent a fiscal drag, but we see limited scope for new tax cuts as the fiscal backdrop has worsened. Broad tariffs could weigh on growth and push up prices, though we expect more targeted tariffs will ultimately be used.
  • All together, we see moderate growth in the U.S. economy in 2025 as these policy shifts play out. Economic fundamentals remain solid, with strong household and corporate balance sheets. The Federal Reserve (Fed) will likely ease policy further toward a neutral setting, but tariffs could slow the pace of rate cuts by interrupting the disinflationary trend. The U.S. economy should remain a global outperformer, though we expect continued bifurcation across sectors, particularly as new policies begin to have an impact.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Please see page 2 for additional performance information.

Bps (basis points): One basis point is equal to 0.01%. Carry: The difference between the cost of financing an asset and the interest received on that asset. Dry powder: highly liquid assets, such as cash or money market instruments, that can be invested when more attractive investment opportunities arise. Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors. In general, the value of a fixed-income security falls when interest rates rise and rises when interest rates fall. Longer term bonds are more sensitive to interest rate changes and subject to greater volatility than those with shorter maturities. During periods of declining rates, the interest rates on floating rate securities generally reset downward and their value is unlikely to rise to the same extent as comparable fixed rate securities.

Investors in asset-backed securities, including collateralized loan obligations (“CLOs”), generally receive payments that are part interest and part return of principal. These payments may vary based on the rate loans are repaid. Some asset-backed securities may have structures that make their reaction to interest rates and other factors difficult to predict, making their prices volatile and they are subject to liquidity and valuation risk.

High yield and unrated debt securities are at a greater risk of default than investment grade bonds and may be less liquid, which may increase volatility.

When market conditions are deemed appropriate, the fund will leverage to the full extent permitted by its investment policies and restrictions and applicable law. Leveraging will exaggerate the effect on net asset value of any increase or decrease in the market value of the fund’s portfolio.

The fund may invest in derivative instruments, which may be more volatile and less liquid, increasing the risk of loss when compared to traditional securities. Certain of the derivative instruments are also subject to the risks of counterparty default and adverse tax treatment.

Instruments and strategies (such as borrowing transactions and reverse repurchase agreements) may provide leveraged exposure to a particular investment, which will magnify any gains or losses on those investments.

Investments in reverse repurchase agreements expose the fund to the many of the same risks as investments in derivatives. The fund’s investments in other investment vehicles subject the fund to those risks and expenses affecting the investment vehicle.

The fund’s investments in foreign securities carry additional risks when compared to U.S. securities, due to the impact of diplomatic, political, or economic developments in the country in question (investments in emerging markets securities are generally subject to an even greater level of risks).

Investments in syndicated bank loans generally offer a floating interest rate and involve special types of risks.

The fund’s investments in municipal securities can be affected by events that affect the municipal bond market.

The fund’s investments in real estate securities subject the fund to the same risks as direct investments in real estate, which is particularly sensitive to economic downturns.

The fund’s investments in restricted securities may involve financial and liquidity risk.

You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares.

It is important to note that the fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. government. Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

Index Definition. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond 1–3 Year Total Return Index measures the performance of publicly issued investment grade corporate, U.S. Treasury and government agency securities with remaining maturities of one to three years.

This material is not intended as a recommendation or as investment advice of any kind, including in connection with rollovers, transfers, and distributions. Such material is not provided in a fiduciary capacity, may not be relied upon for or in connection with the making of investment decisions, and does not constitute a solicitation of an offer to buy or sell securities. All content has been provided for informational or educational purposes only and is not intended to be and should not be construed as legal or tax advice and/or a legal opinion. Always consult a financial, tax, and/or legal professional regarding your specific situation.

Read the fund’s prospectus and summary prospectus (if available) carefully before investing. It contains the fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges, expenses, and other information, which should be considered carefully before investing. Obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus (if available) at GuggenheimInvestments.com.

The referenced fund is distributed by Guggenheim Funds Distributors, LLC. Guggenheim Investments represents the investment management businesses of Guggenheim Partners, LLC (“Guggenheim”), which includes Guggenheim Partners Investment Management (“GPIM”), the investment advisor to the referenced fund. Guggenheim Funds Distributors, LLC, is affiliated with Guggenheim and GPIM.

data.title: Weighing The Hammer
data.description:
Another spasmodic séance gripped Wall Street today as investors squinted over the tariff tarot cards. Stocks staged a 3% levitation act this morning before fading to the close, landing close enough to a bear market to count the fillings in its teeth.

Level Change 4/8/25 (%)

-0.8 Dow

-2.2 Nasdaq

-2.0 Nasdaq 100

-1.6 S&P 500

-2.2 S&P 400

-3.0 S&P 600

Even volatility has been volatile. The VIX popped to 60 yesterday, slipped under 40 this morning in a bit of false calm, then lunged back to 52 by the close.

“Classic bear market behavior,” say analysts, between sips of antacid. Indeed. Since February 19, the S&P 500 is down 19%, while the Nasdaq, ever the overachiever, has shed 24%.

It’s all about tariffs, naturally. Investors are still trying to get their arms around Trump’s trade war, but keep ending up in a bear hug with economic slowdown and inflation.

Stocks perked up this morning on signs that Team Trump may finally be catching on: in the saber-rattling business, it helps to look like you’re willing to sheath one now and then. Both Treasury Secretary Bessent and National Economic Council head Hassett made conciliatory noises, name-dropping Japan and South Korea as potential partners in dialogue.

Trump said China “wants to make a deal.” He’s waiting by the phone - and might be there a while. China’s Commerce Ministry said it would “fight to the end.”

Investors gulped. The end of what?

Then it all came unglued around 10:30, when the White House confirmed a 104% tariff on China will take effect tomorrow, punishment for failing to remove its retaliation against US retaliatory tariffs.

The 104% figure struck onlookers as rather steep, more like a ransom demand than a tariff. “I’ve seen bribes smaller than that,” one trader was overheard telling a colleague clutching a stress ball.

In testimony to the Senate finance committee, US Trade Rep Jamieson Greer defended the administration’s sweeping tariffs. He said the US must “move away from an economy that’s based solely on the financial sector and government spending” to one that produces “real goods and services.”

Ron Wyden (D-OR) countered that the “aimless” and “chaotic” tariff rollout took the US economy “from the envy of the world to a laughingstock in less time than it took to finish March Madness.” Thom Tillis (R-NC) asked, “Whose throat do I get to choke if this proves to be wrong?”

Greer assured senators that Trump’s tariffs are already bearing negotiation fruit, adding that, no, there won’t be any exemptions or exclusions. These are tariffs for all seasons.

Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said the tariffs are “way bigger” than anything the Fed modeled. His recent talks with business people found anxiety rising faster than a teenager’s temperature when the wi-fi goes out: “The anxiety is this is just going to take us back to a thing we spent the last five years desperately trying to get away from.” No, not Trump - inflation.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly repeated the one about policy being in a good place for the Fed to “tread slowly,” but she worries about inflation.

Sentiment at the National Federation of Independent Business has been way ahead of the curve - unfortunately, the curve of despondence. It fell for the third straight month in March, this time by 3.3 points to 97.4, just below the 51-year average of 98.

The release noted that small businesses have reined in sales growth expectations, thanks to the fog surrounding whatever it is Washington currently calls policy implementation.

Don’t give up hope just yet. Recent jumpiness, plus the S&P’s double-dip into bear market territory and back out, suggests investors are ready to stampede back into stocks on any good trade news.

Keep your fingers crossed for some.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
